message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 17
Recent Green phase: Added the loyalty discount (Tier 2 policy-wide). Introduced a Customer interface { yearsWithMHPCO: number } and typed quote's first parameter. policyWideModifiers(policyBase, customer) now returns FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE*policyBase - loyaltyDiscount(policyBase, customer), where loyaltyDiscount applies 0.2*policyBase when yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS (2).

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green. Run tests with `pnpm test`. The next upcoming policy-wide modifier is the follow-up contract discount (-15% of policy base when quoteIndex >= 1, i.e. every quote after the customer's first). Do NOT implement it yet, but you may consider whether policyWideModifiers' shape will accommodate it cleanly. If already clean, document why.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 17 tests pass. Now let me perform the refactoring analysis.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

The Green phase added `loyaltyDiscount` and modified `policyWideModifiers`. Let me evaluate the names:

- `loyaltyDiscount(policyBase, customer)` — clearly reveals intent (a discount based on loyalty). Good.
- `policyWideModifiers(policyBase, customer)` — accurately describes Tier 2 modifiers computed off the policy base. Good.
- `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT` — a constant rate (0.2). The name reads like an amount, not a rate. The sibling rate constants are `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE`, `CURSE_SURCHARGE`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE` — all named `*_SURCHARGE` for rates. `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT` breaks that pattern (other rate constants don't carry the `_RATE` suffix, so the analogous name is `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT`, but it's slightly ambiguous vs. the function `loyaltyDiscount`). This is minor; the existing convention favors `<NAME>_<KIND>` for rates, so `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT` is consistent enough. No rename needed.

Names are clear. No rename required.

## Step 2: Current APP Mass — focus on the Green-phase code

Let me measure the two functions touched in the Green phase.

`loyaltyDiscount`:
```
customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS ? LOYALTY_DISCOUNT * policyBase : 0
```
- Bindings: `policyBase`, `customer`, `customer.yearsWithMHPCO`, `LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS`, `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT`, `policyBase` = 6
- Constants: `0` = 1
- Invocations: `*` = 2
- Conditionals: `?:` = 4
- **Subtotal: 13**

`policyWideModifiers`:
```
FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE * policyBase - loyaltyDiscount(policyBase, customer)
```
- Bindings: `policyBase`, `customer`, `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE`, `policyBase`, `policyBase`, `customer` = 6
- Invocations: `*`(2) + `-`(2) + `loyaltyDiscount()`(2) = 6
- **Subtotal: 12**

## Step 3: Apply the Four Rules

**Rule 1 (Tests pass):** All 17 green.

**Rule 2 (Reveals intent):** The `policyWideModifiers` body subtracts a discount from a surcharge inline. The comment promises Tier 2 will host "first-insurance surcharge; later loyalty/follow-up discounts." The current shape — `surcharge - discount` — is a flat arithmetic expression. When the follow-up discount arrives, it becomes `surcharge - loyaltyDiscount - followUpDiscount`, which is still readable but the structure doesn't make the "sum of independent modifiers" concept explicit. There's a latent conceptual duplication: `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE * policyBase` is itself a policy-wide modifier expressed differently from `loyaltyDiscount(policyBase, ...)`.

**Rule 3 (No duplication):** The first-insurance surcharge is computed inline as `rate * policyBase`, while loyalty is a named function `loyaltyDiscount(policyBase, customer)`. These are the same *kind* of thing (a fraction of policy base) expressed two ways — conceptual duplication. Extracting a sibling `firstInsuranceSurcharge(policyBase)` makes the two modifiers symmetric and makes `policyWideModifiers` read as a composition of named modifiers.

**Rule 4 (Fewest elements):** Extraction adds one element but improves symmetry and accommodates the next modifier cleanly.

## Step 4: Implement Refactoring

I'll extract `firstInsuranceSurcharge` so both Tier 2 modifiers are named functions of `(policyBase, customer)` shape, making `policyWideModifiers` a clean sum/difference of named modifiers. This directly addresses the prompt's concern about accommodating the follow-up discount.

content.text:
All 17 tests still pass.

## Step 5: New APP Mass

`firstInsuranceSurcharge` (new):
```
FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE * policyBase
```
- Bindings: `policyBase`, `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE`, `policyBase` = 3
- Invocations: `*` = 2
- **Subtotal: 5**

`policyWideModifiers` (refactored):
```
firstInsuranceSurcharge(policyBase) - loyaltyDiscount(policyBase, customer)
```
- Bindings: `policyBase`, `customer`, `policyBase`, `policyBase`, `customer` = 5
- Invocations: `firstInsuranceSurcharge()`(2) + `-`(2) + `loyaltyDiscount()`(2) = 6
- **Subtotal: 11**

Combined touched-code mass:
- Before: `policyWideModifiers` 12 (loyaltyDiscount 13 unchanged)
- After: `policyWideModifiers` 11 + `firstInsuranceSurcharge` 5 = 16 (loyaltyDiscount 13 unchanged)

Mass rose by +4 in absolute terms (one new named function), but `policyWideModifiers` itself dropped 12 → 11. This is the documented Rule 2/3-over-APP tradeoff: the small mass increase buys conceptual symmetry between the two Tier 2 modifiers and removes the conceptual duplication (inline rate vs. named function).

## Step 6: Decision & accommodating the next modifier

**Refactoring applied:**
- Rule 3 (No duplication): Extracted `firstInsuranceSurcharge(policyBase)` so the first-insurance surcharge is a named function like `loyaltyDiscount`, removing the inline-vs-named conceptual duplication of "fraction of policy base."
- Rule 2 (Reveals intent): `policyWideModifiers` now reads as a composition of named modifiers — `firstInsuranceSurcharge(...) - loyaltyDiscount(...)` — rather than mixing raw arithmetic with a function call.

**Will the shape accommodate the upcoming follow-up discount cleanly?** Yes. The follow-up discount is "-15% of policy base when `quoteIndex >= 1`." It will be a sibling function `followUpDiscount(policyBase, quoteIndex)` of the exact same fraction-of-base shape, and `policyWideModifiers` extends by one subtracted term:
```
firstInsuranceSurcharge(policyBase)
  - loyaltyDiscount(policyBase, customer)
  - followUpDiscount(policyBase, quoteIndex)
```
The only seam to note: `policyWideModifiers` currently takes `(policyBase, customer)`. The follow-up discount depends on `quoteIndex` (already a `quote` parameter), so that signature will need a third argument when the discount is implemented. I deliberately did NOT add it now — adding an unused parameter would violate Rule 4 (fewest elements) and YAGNI. The extension is a one-line signature change plus one subtracted term, which is clean.

---

## Refactoring Complete

**Refactoring:** Extracted `firstInsuranceSurcharge(policyBase)` to make both Tier 2 policy-wide modifiers named, symmetric functions; `policyWideModifiers` now composes them. Removes conceptual duplication (Rule 3) and improves intent (Rule 2).

**Mass change:** `policyWideModifiers` 12 → 11; net touched-code +4 (one new named function). Accepted tradeoff — Rule 2/3 clarity over raw APP mass, as the rules prescribe.

**Naming:** Evaluated first; all names clear, no renames needed.

**Next modifier:** The follow-up discount fits the established shape cleanly — add a `followUpDiscount(policyBase, quoteIndex)` sibling and one subtracted term; `policyWideModifiers` will need `quoteIndex` added to its signature. Not implemented now (YAGNI / Rule 4).

**Tests:** All 17 passing.

File: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_19-33-19_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.2-with-why-cleaned_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
